meta.description: A rowdy bar night with heavy drinkers and short attention spans. High signal frequency, lots of phone usage, moderate chaos.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: Ooh okay Pat, did you actually catch someone? I'm voting LEGIT because I literally wasn't paying attention and I respect the energy, plus someone probably interrupted someone in the last five minutes just naturally.
jade.internal_thought: This is fun, we're doing things now, Pat seems energized, I should vote yes and keep the vibe going because everyone winning early makes the night feel better.

pat.dialogue: Whoa, hold on—I literally just called everyone in five minutes ago. Did I interrupt someone? I wasn't even paying attention to the game yet, I was checking if we had enough ice. This feels like a setup.
pat.internal_thought: This is weird and honestly creepy—the AI is claiming I caught an interruption that didn't happen, and it's making me feel paranoid about being watched, which is the OPPOSITE of what I want tonight.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that's legit. I saw it happen like thirty seconds ago.
river.internal_thought: Pat definitely cut off Alex mid-sentence. Easy call. Wait—does this AI actually know I'm watching everyone? That's... creepy but also kind of impressive.

votingMechanic.name: The Auction
votingMechanic.description: Bid your own points. Highest bidder decides the outcome.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'm claiming this one. And honestly? I'm a little freaked out by how well these missions know us. That's impressive and weird at the same time.
pat.internal_thought: The AI is definitely reading the room in a way that feels almost too accurate, and part of me loves it and part of me is genuinely unsettled—but I can't let that distract me from managing the night.

river.dialogue: Pat's been quiet. That's... actually a solid claim if they stuck with it. The auction thing is kind of weird though.
river.internal_thought: Pat's annoyed and just grabbed 10 points out of nowhere — they were watching too, same way I watch. That's fine. But this AI reading-your-mind thing feels less thrilling and more like it knows too much about how I operate, which is exactly the point of being an observer: nobody's supposed to notice.

timeline.title: Compliment Bomb
timeline.description: First person to give a genuine compliment to the player on their left wins.

alex.dialogue: Oh THIS is happening right now? Perfect — hey River, you're on my left, right? That shirt actually looks way better on you than it did last time, no cap.
alex.internal_thought: This is soft and boring compared to what I could make happen, but I'm absolutely claiming it because 20 points is free money and I can weaponize genuine compliments to build allies right before I burn them later.
alex.submission_if_applicable: River — honestly your energy tonight is contagious, I meant that.

jade.dialogue: Oh MY GOD yes, okay okay—River you're on my left right? Dude, I genuinely love how you always ask the smart questions nobody else thinks to ask, like you actually care about understanding stuff instead of just vibing, and that's such a rare thing.
jade.internal_thought: This is literally what I'm here for—fast, social, everybody wins energy, and the fact that it's timed means I gotta move NOW instead of overthinking it.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, uh—compliment the person on my left? Yeah, okay, I got this. Wait, who's on my left again?
tyler.internal_thought: This is actually kinda fun but also lowkey weird that the AI is already reading us—did it really notice I hate chaos? Whatever, 20 points is solid if I don't mess it up.

pat.dialogue: Oh, that's a good one—nice and quick. Everyone knows what they're doing, no confusion. Let's go, left side, who's up first?
pat.internal_thought: This is exactly the right kind of event right now—low stakes, high participation, gets everyone talking to each other instead of at each other, and Jade's already got that energy to carry it. Perfect pacing.

river.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually pretty good. Tyler's to my left. Hey Tyler — you've got that thing where you commit to every bit, even the bad ones, and somehow it still lands. That's rare.
river.internal_thought: Wait, did the AI just hand me a perfect setup for what I'm actually good at? This feels weirdly calculated and I kind of hate that it works.

votingMechanic.name: The Alibi
votingMechanic.description: Claimant and a random witness both tell the story. Do they match?

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.dialogue: Oh FUCK yeah, Pat's going for it — okay okay, so who's the witness? Because I'm already mentally replaying the last five minutes trying to remember if Pat actually swore or if this is a blatant bullshit claim, and honestly? That's the whole game right there.
alex.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the chaos I came for — Pat's either genuinely caught someone or they're bluffing hard, and the Alibi mechanic is perfect because now we get to see if the witness corroborates or if Pat gets exposed. This is the storytelling moment.

jade.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat, okay so who was it? I need to hear this story because I was definitely not paying attention like two minutes ago, but I'm already invested. Spill!
jade.internal_thought: This is perfect, Pat getting points, more drama, this is literally why I'm here—watching people win tiny victories is everything.

pat.dialogue: Oh, I'm totally claiming this—I definitely said something with a little spice in it during setup, and honestly? I'm impressed this thing caught it. That's some solid monitoring.
pat.internal_thought: The AI is definitely reading us. That's either brilliant or creepy, but right now it's working—everyone's locked in, energy is climbing, and we're only 8 minutes in with three solid events already.
pat.submission_if_applicable: I said something about 'this better not be a disaster' when I was setting up the snack table. River heard it.

river.dialogue: Pat caught someone already? Alright, let's hear it then.
river.internal_thought: Pat's being weirdly aggressive tonight, and yeah, the AI is definitely reading the room in a way that feels like it's watching us specifically — which is either clever or creepy depending on whether this stays fun.

votingMechanic.name: THE DICTATOR
votingMechanic.description: One player has been chosen. Their word is law.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: Oh dude, Pat—okay so I'm the dictator? That's INSANE. Pat, did you actually keep a straight face? Because if you did that's legendary, but also I need to know the context because I was obviously mid-conversation like always.
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly why I came tonight—everyone's vibing, Pat's got a claim, and I get to decide? This is perfect chaos energy and I'm so here for it.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, so Pat's claiming they kept a straight face? I mean... I wasn't really watching, but sure, whatever.
tyler.internal_thought: Pat definitely laughed at something earlier, but honestly I was checking my phone—did anyone else see it clearly or am I supposed to just guess here?

pat.dialogue: Oh man, I'm claiming this—and you know what? I absolutely kept my face straight when Tyler almost fell off the couch during setup. I was DEAD inside but didn't crack.
pat.internal_thought: This AI thing is actually terrifying in the best way possible—it's reading us like it's been sitting here all night, and the fact that it caught Straight Face for me specifically when I've been running on pure host-mode poker face is... kind of genius, actually.

river.dialogue: Wait, hold up — Pat's claiming they kept a straight face? That's bold. Who's the dictator?
river.internal_thought: Pat's been genuinely quiet, which actually tracks... but I need to see who decides this before I care.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

alex.dialogue: Okay wait, Jade — you're claiming Topic Hijacker? Because if you actually steered us somewhere weird I genuinely didn't notice, that's actually impressive and you get my vote, but if this is a Hail Mary bullshit claim I'm absolutely calling it.
alex.internal_thought: Jade's energy is stupid high and they're in full chaos mode like me, so either they actually pulled this off smooth as hell or they're making a desperate play — either way this is the kind of ambiguous-as-hell claim that makes the night actually memorable.

jade.dialogue: YES—oh my god, OKAY, so like five minutes ago River asked about the rule clarification and I was like 'wait what are we even talking about?' and then Pat made that joke about the weird name and I literally steered us into talking about bad celebrity baby names for like two minutes straight and EVERYONE was laughing at how ridiculous it was—that was totally me, right?
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of thing I live for—getting caught doing something genuinely social and chaotic that actually happened, and now I'm getting POINTS for it? This night is perfect and also wait... how did the AI know that happened? That's weirdly specific but also kind of sick, honestly.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming that? I mean... did she actually do it? I wasn't really paying attention to the conversation.
tyler.internal_thought: I have no idea if Jade steered the conversation or not because I was scrolling my fantasy league like ten minutes ago.

pat.dialogue: Okay Jade, you've got fifteen seconds—what's your pitch? And honestly, I'm dying to know which random topic you actually steered us toward.
pat.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of mission that makes tonight work—active, requires real-time reading of the room, and Jade's been so locked in that she probably nailed it; also this AI thing is genuinely unsettling but in a way that makes me want to keep playing.

river.dialogue: Okay, Jade — what'd you do? Because I actually watched you steer us off the rails like twice already.
river.internal_thought: Jade's been doing the social thing all night, and honestly yeah, I remember when she pivoted us hard from that Tyler story into the Alex thing... let me actually listen to what she says before I decide.

recommendations: Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Drawing mini-games generated the most screenshot-worthy moments (0% engagement). Increase frequency.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 80% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Oh FUCK yeah, Pat's going for it — okay okay, so who's the witness? Because I'm already mentally replaying the last five minutes trying to remember if Pat actually swore or if this is a blatant bullshit claim, and honestly? That's the whole game right there.

highlights.title: Compliment Bomb
highlights.dialogue: Oh THIS is happening right now? Perfect — hey River, you're on my left, right? That shirt actually looks way better on you than it did last time, no cap.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Okay wait, Jade — you're claiming Topic Hijacker? Because if you actually steered us somewhere weird I genuinely didn't notice, that's actually impressive and you get my vote, but if this is a Hail Mary bullshit claim I'm absolutely calling it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay wait, Jade — you're claiming Topic Hijacker? Because if you actually steered us somewhere weird I genuinely didn't notice, that's actually impressive and you get my vote, but if this is a Hail Mary bullshit claim I'm absolutely calling it.

lowPoints.title: Compliment Bomb
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh THIS is happening right now? Perfect — hey River, you're on my left, right? That shirt actually looks way better on you than it did last time, no cap.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh FUCK yeah, Pat's going for it — okay okay, so who's the witness? Because I'm already mentally replaying the last five minutes trying to remember if Pat actually swore or if this is a blatant bullshit claim, and honestly? That's the whole game right there.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one flash mission all night when there was clearly room for more chaos to unfold.
assessment.best_moment: Pat's Potty Mouth claim where I got to immediately audition the entire group as potential witnesses and decide in real-time whether to burn them or let it slide.
assessment.narrative_summary: Two hours of exactly what I needed — a system begging to be tested and a group willing to let me push it. I came in hot, found the fault lines immediately (Jade's Topic Hijacker claim was the real test), and watched everyone decide whether to trust me or call bullshit. Ended stronger than I started because the group kept matching my energy instead of shutting it down. This is the kind of night people reference when they need an excuse to get us all together again.
suggestions: Trigger flash missions more aggressively — one in 120 minutes is leaving meat on the bone
Let claims stand longer before resolution so the group actually debates harder instead of resolving fast
Add an event type that rewards explicitly false claims that people believe — right now honesty and bullshit get treated the same
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Me calling out Jade's Topic Hijacker claim as either genius or a blatant Hail Mary with no middle ground, then the whole table having to collectively decide if she was that subtle or just that desperate — nobody actually knew and that ambiguity is the whole point.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Compliment Bomb
highlights.dialogue: Oh MY GOD yes, okay okay—River you're on my left right? Dude, I genuinely love how you always ask the smart questions nobody else thinks to ask, like you actually care about understanding stuff instead of just vibing, and that's such a rare thing.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: YES—oh my god, OKAY, so like five minutes ago River asked about the rule clarification and I was like 'wait what are we even talking about?' and then Pat made that joke about the weird name and I literally steered us into talking about bad celebrity baby names for like two minutes straight and EVERYONE was laughing at how ridiculous it was—that was totally me, right?

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat, okay so who was it? I need to hear this story because I was definitely not paying attention like two minutes ago, but I'm already invested. Spill!

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Ooh okay Pat, did you actually catch someone? I'm voting LEGIT because I literally wasn't paying attention and I respect the energy, plus someone probably interrupted someone in the last five minutes just naturally.

lowPoints.title: Compliment Bomb
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh MY GOD yes, okay okay—River you're on my left right? Dude, I genuinely love how you always ask the smart questions nobody else thinks to ask, like you actually care about understanding stuff instead of just vibing, and that's such a rare thing.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat, okay so who was it? I need to hear this story because I was definitely not paying attention like two minutes ago, but I'm already invested. Spill!

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly nothing—I was too busy having fun with everyone to notice anything wrong.
assessment.best_moment: When I got to completely derail us into talking about ridiculous celebrity baby names and literally everyone was losing it.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was exactly what I needed—a solid two hours of just being WITH these people and watching each other light up over stupid stuff. The chaos agent kept throwing us these little moments to claim and celebrate, and I went all in on every single one because why wouldn't I? Ended up finishing third but honestly who cares when River was laughing that hard at my compliment and Pat was trying to tell that story nobody was following. That's the whole game right there.
suggestions: Keep the flash missions coming—they're the perfect size for getting everyone hyped without derailing the vibe
Maybe throw in more opportunities for group celebrations, not just individual claims
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat asking who swore and me just immediately being like 'I wasn't paying attention but I'm INVESTED, spill everything'—and then finding out it was some tiny thing that happened forever ago and none of us could even remember the context anymore.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Compliment Bomb
highlights.dialogue: Oh, uh—compliment the person on my left? Yeah, okay, I got this. Wait, who's on my left again?

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Wait, so Pat's claiming they kept a straight face? I mean... I wasn't really watching, but sure, whatever.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming that? I mean... did she actually do it? I wasn't really paying attention to the conversation.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Straight Face
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, so Pat's claiming they kept a straight face? I mean... I wasn't really watching, but sure, whatever.

lowPoints.title: Compliment Bomb
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, uh—compliment the person on my left? Yeah, okay, I got this. Wait, who's on my left again?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming that? I mean... did she actually do it? I wasn't really paying attention to the conversation.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly missed half the stuff that was happening because I couldn't keep my eyes off my phone, and that made it hard to actually vote on anything without just guessing.
assessment.best_moment: When I finally caught that compliment thing at the start and actually knew what was going on for like 10 seconds.
assessment.narrative_summary: So like, we did this game night thing for two hours and it was... fine? There were these random events popping up that I was supposed to pay attention to, but I kept getting distracted by texts and my fantasy league blowing up. I caught a few of the challenges and tried to play along, but mostly I was just saying 'wait, what happened?' like an idiot. Scored pretty low because I honestly wasn't locked in the whole time, but the people were cool so it wasn't a total waste.
suggestions: Maybe make the events harder to miss — like they need full phone-down attention or something
The standing missions felt kinda slow, speed them up or cut them
Shorter night might be better — 2 hours is a long time to stay focused on a party game
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I asked who was on my left during the compliment thing and literally everyone looked at me confused — like dude, you've been sitting here the whole time.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Oh man, I'm claiming this—and you know what? I absolutely kept my face straight when Tyler almost fell off the couch during setup. I was DEAD inside but didn't crack.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Okay Jade, you've got fifteen seconds—what's your pitch? And honestly, I'm dying to know which random topic you actually steered us toward.

highlights.title: Compliment Bomb
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's a good one—nice and quick. Everyone knows what they're doing, no confusion. Let's go, left side, who's up first?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Whoa, hold on—I literally just called everyone in five minutes ago. Did I interrupt someone? I wasn't even paying attention to the game yet, I was checking if we had enough ice. This feels like a setup.

lowPoints.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'm claiming this one. And honestly? I'm a little freaked out by how well these missions know us. That's impressive and weird at the same time.

lowPoints.title: Compliment Bomb
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, that's a good one—nice and quick. Everyone knows what they're doing, no confusion. Let's go, left side, who's up first?

assessment.biggest_complaint: That opening 'Interrupter' claim felt like a cheap shot—I was literally still checking on logistics and suddenly I'm in the game before I even had my head in it.
assessment.best_moment: When I kept my face completely stone during Tyler's near-disaster at minute 9—that was peak control and everyone lost it when I revealed I was holding it together the whole time.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started rough with that early snipe about interrupting, but once I settled in, this thing became exactly what I needed—a way to keep everyone engaged and laughing without anyone feeling singled out. The game knew just how to poke fun at us in ways that landed, and I got to do what I love: orchestrate a night people will actually remember. Energy stayed high, no real conflicts, and I ended up leading the pack without it feeling forced.
suggestions: Maybe give hosts a 2-3 minute buffer before the game starts calling claims—let us actually get people seated first
The weirdness factor of how well the AI read us was cool but slightly unsettling; consider adding a 'tone' setting so hosts can dial in how personal the observations feel
I'd love an option to see which moments the AI flagged as most likely to create tension, so I can prep my mediation instincts
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Tyler almost eating it off the couch during setup and me having to sit there completely deadpan while everyone else panicked—then later when I claimed I was dead inside the whole time, the table absolutely lost it. That's the kind of moment that becomes a inside joke for months.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Compliment Bomb
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually pretty good. Tyler's to my left. Hey Tyler — you've got that thing where you commit to every bit, even the bad ones, and somehow it still lands. That's rare.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up — Pat's claiming they kept a straight face? That's bold. Who's the dictator?

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Okay, Jade — what'd you do? Because I actually watched you steer us off the rails like twice already.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat caught someone already? Alright, let's hear it then.

lowPoints.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat's been quiet. That's... actually a solid claim if they stuck with it. The auction thing is kind of weird though.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, that's legit. I saw it happen like thirty seconds ago.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I scored zero because the game mechanic doesn't actually reward paying attention — you just watch, react, and forget about it.
assessment.best_moment: Tyler committing fully to every bad bit and somehow still landing it — that's the kind of thing that actually matters in a room.
assessment.narrative_summary: Spent most of the night observing while everyone else chased claims and chaos events. I engaged with everything that came up because why not, but the whole scoring system felt hollow — zero points for actually catching real moments. The night had decent energy and people were loose, but the game itself didn't add much beyond what was already there. Felt like we were playing *for* the app instead of playing *with* it.
suggestions: Make observation actually matter — reward people who catch real behavior instead of just claiming random traits.
Cut the event frequency in half; six interruptions in 120 minutes breaks whatever natural rhythm you had going.
Let standing missions breathe — they're the only thing that feels like it matters.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat's straight-face claim right after already getting caught on something else — the audacity to keep going was kind of perfect, but nobody needs an app to appreciate that kind of persistence.
